locked_facts: Inductance of solenoid: L = μ₀n²Al (n = turns/length, A = area, l = length)
Back EMF opposes change in current (electromagnetic inertia)
LR circuit growth: I(t) = (V/R)(1 - e^(-t/τ)) where τ = L/R
LR circuit decay: I(t) = I₀ e^(-t/τ)
Energy stored in inductor: U = ½LI²
Time constant τ = L/R: after 5τ, current reaches ~99% of final value
Inductance is the electrical analogue of inertia — resists changes in current
truth_statements: Self-inductance causes a coil to oppose any change in the current flowing through it by generating a back EMF
The back EMF is proportional to the rate of change of current: ε = -LdI/dt
In an LR circuit the current grows exponentially toward V/R with time constant τ = L/R
The energy stored in the magnetic field of an inductor is ½LI²
A larger inductance means the current takes longer to reach its steady-state value

aha_moment.what_to_show: An LR circuit switched on: current doesn't jump to V/R instantly — it grows exponentially. A graph shows I vs t rising with time constant τ = L/R. Simultaneously, the back EMF starts at V and decays to 0. The inductor acts like electromagnetic inertia.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Current growth curve and back EMF decay curve shown simultaneously — the inductor 'resists' change then gradually allows current through
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students expect instant current flow — seeing the exponential growth with time constant makes inductor behaviour tangible

simulation_states.label: Self-inductance concept
simulation_states.description: Current through a coil creates magnetic flux. Changing I changes Φ. Back EMF = -LdI/dt opposes the change. Analogy: mass resists acceleration, inductor resists current change.
simulation_states.emphasis: An inductor resists CHANGES in current — it's electromagnetic inertia

simulation_states.label: LR circuit switch-on
simulation_states.description: Battery, resistor R, inductor L in series. Switch closed at t = 0. Current starts at 0, grows exponentially to V/R. Back EMF starts at V, decays to 0. Time constant τ = L/R.
simulation_states.emphasis: Current can't jump instantly — the inductor fights the change

simulation_states.label: Growth and decay curves
simulation_states.description: Switch-on: I rises as (1-e^(-t/τ)). Switch-off: I decays as e^(-t/τ). Both curves shown. At t = τ, I reaches 63% (growth) or drops to 37% (decay). At 5τ, essentially complete.
simulation_states.emphasis: τ = L/R controls the speed. Large L or small R = slow response.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts L and R. Time constant changes. Switch on/off and watch current grow/decay. Energy bar shows U = ½LI² building up. Compare different L/R ratios.
simulation_states.emphasis: Increase L — current grows slower. Increase R — current grows faster (smaller τ).

common_misconceptions.correction: An inductor opposes CHANGES in current, not current itself. Once steady state is reached (dI/dt = 0), the inductor acts like a plain wire with zero resistance (ideal inductor). It only generates back EMF during transients.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show current eventually reaching V/R — the inductor allows full current in steady state

common_misconceptions.belief: Time constant τ should be large for fast response
common_misconceptions.correction: Larger τ = L/R means SLOWER response. The current takes longer to reach its final value. For fast switching, you want small L or large R (small τ).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Compare circuits with different τ — larger τ curve rises much more slowly
